text.title: Shadow Handover Session Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn an offshore-to-onshore handover from a demo into a verified transfer of operational ownership.

rule.statement: The receiver MUST drive the keyboard, terminal, and decisions for the entire session; the outgoing engineer never touches input devices except to recover an explicitly-stuck session.

rationale.source: Knowledge-transfer cohort study; Bunderson and Sutcliffe expertise research

rule.rationale: The receiver-does / outgoing-watches inversion is the whole point — when the outgoing engineer drives, the receiver memorises ritual without internalising mechanism.

rule.statement: The outgoing engineer MUST stay silent by default; they answer only when the receiver explicitly asks a question. No spontaneous "actually you also need to...".

rationale.source: Apprenticeship-learning research; Dreyfus model of skill acquisition

rule.rationale: Spontaneous corrections rob the receiver of the chance to fail and recover, which is the only durable form of knowledge transfer.

rule.statement: Every question the receiver asks MUST be logged verbatim in a written gap log with answer + linked permanent doc; questions without a doc link are open action items.
rule.rationale: Tribal knowledge is the set of facts that get answered verbally and never written down. Forcing each question into a doc-or-action captures it one session at a time.

rule.statement: The session MUST end with a written two-party sign-off naming (a) tasks the receiver successfully drove unaided, (b) tasks needing a second session, (c) any single-point-of-failure facts not yet documented.

rationale.source: ITIL knowledge-handover guidance; PMI project-closure standards

rule.rationale: Without an explicit sign-off, both parties walk away assuming "I think they got it" and the first incident reveals the gap.

rule.statement: The task list MUST be defined before the session starts and the session is NOT considered complete until every task has been driven by the receiver OR explicitly logged as "deferred to session N+1"; tasks are never silently skipped.

rationale.source: Atul Gawande — The Checklist Manifesto; aviation handover protocols

rule.rationale: Skipped tasks are the most common post-mortem finding: "we never actually walked through restore-from-backup".
